front: What is the small file problem in Iceberg, and how does compaction solve it?
back: Streaming writes to Iceberg (e.g., from Flink every 60 seconds) produce many small files — one set per checkpoint interval per partition. Small files hurt read performance because query engines must open thousands of files, incurring per-file overhead (S3 GET requests, Parquet footer reads, JVM overhead). A table receiving 1000 events/minute partitioned by day across 10 Flink tasks writes 10 × 1440 = 14,400 files per day if checkpointing every minute — almost all tiny. Compaction (rewriting) merges small files into larger ones (target: 128MB-512MB per file). In Spark: `SparkActions.get().rewriteDataFiles(table).option('target-file-size-bytes', str(256 * 1024 * 1024)).execute()`. Also rewrite manifests: `SparkActions.get().rewriteManifests(table).execute()` — reduces manifest scanning overhead. Schedule compaction regularly (e.g., hourly for streaming tables, daily for batch). The flink-iceberg connector's auto-compaction feature can trigger compaction within the streaming job itself, though external Spark compaction jobs give more control.
